This provision is triggered when there is a need to balance public access to official documents with the protection of personal data. Organizations must ensure that any disclosure complies with applicable laws and does not infringe on individuals' data protection rights. A common misconception is that all official documents can be freely disclosed, but this is only true if specific legal conditions are met.

scenario: responding to a public records request

Art.86.1.business_description: This provision applies when a public authority or body holds official documents containing personal data. It allows these entities to disclose such data if permitted by EU or Member State law, balancing public access to information with data protection rights. Organizations must ensure that any disclosure aligns with legal requirements and respects individuals' privacy rights. A common misconception is that all official documents can be freely disclosed, but this is only true if specific legal conditions are met. Organizations should carefully assess the legal basis for disclosure to avoid unauthorized data sharing.

Art.87.business_description: Article 87 of the GDPR allows EU Member States to set specific rules for processing national identification numbers or similar identifiers. Organizations must ensure that any use of these identifiers is accompanied by appropriate safeguards to protect individuals' rights and freedoms. This requirement is triggered when an organization processes such identifiers, and it mandates compliance with national laws that may impose additional conditions. A common misconception is that GDPR alone dictates all rules for ID numbers, but national laws play a crucial role here.

scenario: using national ID for customer verification

Art.87.1.business_description: This article allows EU Member States to set specific rules for processing national identification numbers or similar identifiers. Organizations must ensure that any use of these identifiers complies with the safeguards outlined in the GDPR to protect individuals' rights and freedoms. This requirement is triggered when a national ID number is processed, and organizations must follow any additional national laws that apply. A common misconception is that the GDPR alone dictates all rules for ID numbers, but national laws can impose further conditions.

scenario: using national ID for customer verification
processing ID numbers for employee records

Art.88.business_description: Article 88 of the GDPR allows EU Member States to create specific rules for handling employees' personal data in the workplace. This is relevant when processing data for hiring, managing employment contracts, workplace planning, promoting equality, and ensuring health and safety. Organizations must comply with these national rules, which may vary, to protect employees' rights and freedoms. A common misconception is that GDPR alone governs employee data processing, but national laws may impose additional requirements.

Art.88.1.business_description: This article allows EU Member States to establish specific rules for handling employees' personal data in the workplace. It applies when organizations process personal data for employment-related activities like hiring, managing work, and ensuring workplace safety. Organizations must comply with any additional national laws or collective agreements that provide more detailed requirements. A common misconception is that GDPR alone governs employee data processing, but local laws may impose further obligations.

Art.89.business_description: Article 89 of the GDPR requires organizations processing personal data for archiving in the public interest, scientific or historical research, or statistical purposes to implement safeguards that protect the rights and freedoms of individuals. This is triggered when data is used for these specific purposes. Organizations must ensure they have technical and organizational measures in place, focusing on data minimization, which means only collecting and retaining data necessary for these purposes. A common misconception is that these activities are exempt from GDPR, but they must still comply with its principles, particularly regarding data protection and minimization.

Art.89.1.business_description: This article applies when personal data is processed for archiving in the public interest, scientific or historical research, or statistical purposes. Organizations must implement appropriate safeguards to protect the rights and freedoms of individuals whose data is being processed. This means ensuring that technical and organizational measures are in place, particularly focusing on data minimization. A common misconception is that these activities are exempt from GDPR requirements, but safeguards are still necessary to ensure compliance.

Art.90.business_description: Article 90 allows EU Member States to create specific rules for how data protection authorities can interact with organizations bound by professional secrecy, like lawyers or doctors. This is triggered when a controller or processor is subject to confidentiality obligations. Organizations must balance data protection rights with these secrecy obligations, ensuring that they comply with both. A common misconception is that data protection laws override all secrecy obligations, but this article clarifies that both must be reconciled. Organizations should be aware of their national laws that may impose additional requirements in these situations.

Art.90.1.business_description: This provision allows EU Member States to create specific rules regarding how data protection authorities can interact with organizations bound by professional secrecy, such as lawyers or doctors. It is triggered when a supervisory authority needs to investigate or enforce data protection laws but must also respect the confidentiality obligations of the organization. Organizations must understand that while they have obligations to protect personal data, they also need to maintain confidentiality as required by their professional standards. A common misconception is that data protection laws override all confidentiality obligations, but this provision ensures a balance between the two.

Art.91.business_description: Article 91 of the GDPR allows churches and religious associations in EU Member States to continue using their existing data protection rules, as long as these rules are aligned with the GDPR. This means that if a church or religious community already has comprehensive data protection measures, they can keep using them, but they must ensure these measures meet GDPR standards. This provision is triggered when such organizations are processing personal data and already have established data protection frameworks. It requires them to review and update their rules to ensure compliance with the GDPR, without needing to discard their existing systems entirely.

Art.91.1.business_description: This provision applies when churches or religious associations in an EU Member State have their own comprehensive data protection rules. These organizations can continue using their existing rules for processing personal data, but they must ensure these rules align with the GDPR. This means reviewing and updating their policies to meet GDPR standards. It is triggered when such entities are processing personal data under their own frameworks. A common misconception is that these organizations are exempt from GDPR, but they must comply by aligning their rules with the regulation.

Art.92.business_description: Article 92 of the GDPR allows the European Commission to create delegated acts, which are legal acts that supplement or amend non-essential parts of the legislation. This power is granted indefinitely from May 24, 2016. For organizations, this means that the Commission can make certain changes to the GDPR without needing to go through the full legislative process. This is typically triggered when there is a need to update or clarify specific aspects of the regulation. Organizations should stay informed about any new delegated acts as they may impact compliance requirements. A common misconception is that these acts can change core principles of the GDPR, but they only address non-essential elements.

Art.92.1.business_description: This article grants the European Commission the authority to create delegated acts related to the GDPR. This means that the Commission can make specific legal changes or clarifications without needing to pass new legislation through the full legislative process. For controllers and processors, this implies that they need to stay informed about any new delegated acts that may affect their compliance obligations. The delegation of this power is ongoing and began on 24 May 2016. A common misconception is that these acts require immediate changes to operations, but they often provide further clarification or detail to existing rules.

Art.93.business_description: Article 93 of the GDPR outlines that the European Commission will be supported by a committee when implementing delegated and implementing acts. This means that when the Commission needs to make decisions or adopt measures related to data protection, it consults with a committee as defined by Regulation (EU) No 182/2011. For controllers and processors, this indicates that certain regulatory decisions are made with the input of a specialized committee, ensuring that diverse perspectives are considered. This process is triggered when the Commission needs to adopt specific measures under the GDPR framework. There are no direct actions required from organizations, but understanding this process helps in anticipating regulatory changes.

scenario: adoption of new data protection measures

Art.93.1.business_description: Article 93.1 of the GDPR outlines that the European Commission will be supported by a committee as per Regulation (EU) No 182/2011. This is relevant when the Commission needs to adopt implementing acts related to data protection. For controllers and processors, this means that certain decisions or regulations that affect data protection practices may be influenced by this committee's input. Organizations should be aware that this committee's role is procedural and does not impose direct obligations on them. A common misconception is that this committee directly regulates data processing activities, but its role is advisory to the Commission.

Art.94.business_description: This article indicates that the older Directive 95/46/EC was officially replaced by the GDPR as of May 25, 2018. For organizations, this means that any references to the old directive in their policies, contracts, or documentation should now be understood as references to the GDPR. This transition requires organizations to ensure that their compliance frameworks are aligned with the GDPR's requirements, rather than those of the repealed directive. A common misconception is that some parts of the old directive might still apply, but in reality, the GDPR fully supersedes it.

Art.94.1.business_description: This article indicates that Directive 95/46/EC, which previously governed data protection in the EU, was officially repealed on 25 May 2018. From that date, any references to the old Directive should be understood as referring to the GDPR. For organizations, this means that all data protection practices must align with the GDPR's requirements instead of the old Directive. This transition is crucial for compliance, as it ensures that data processing activities are up to date with the current legal framework. A common misconception is that some parts of the old Directive might still apply, but this is not the case; the GDPR fully replaces it.

Art.95.business_description: Article 95 of the GDPR clarifies that it does not add extra obligations for organizations already governed by Directive 2002/58/EC, which deals with electronic communications services. This means if your organization provides publicly available electronic communications services, you should primarily comply with the specific rules in Directive 2002/58/EC for those services. This article is triggered when your processing activities fall under the scope of both the GDPR and the ePrivacy Directive. A common misconception is that GDPR always adds new requirements, but in this case, it defers to the existing ePrivacy rules.

Art.95.1.business_description: Article 95 of the GDPR clarifies that the regulation does not add extra requirements for organizations already covered by Directive 2002/58/EC, which deals with electronic communications services. This means if your organization is providing publicly available electronic communications services, you should primarily follow the specific obligations under Directive 2002/58/EC for data processing related to these services. This article is triggered when processing activities are already regulated by the ePrivacy Directive, ensuring no overlap or conflict between the two sets of rules. A common misconception is that GDPR always adds new obligations, but here it defers to existing rules under the ePrivacy Directive.

Art.96.business_description: This provision applies to international agreements made by EU Member States before May 24, 2016, that involve transferring personal data to third countries or international organizations. These agreements can continue to be used as long as they were compliant with EU laws at the time they were made. Organizations should review such agreements to ensure they remain valid and compliant with current data protection standards. This is particularly relevant when considering amendments or replacements to these agreements. A common misconception is that all pre-existing agreements are automatically invalid under GDPR, which is not the case if they meet the specified criteria.

Art.96.1.business_description: This article addresses international agreements made by EU Member States before May 24, 2016, that involve transferring personal data to countries outside the EU or to international organizations. These agreements can continue to be valid as long as they complied with EU law at the time they were made. Organizations should review such agreements to ensure they still align with current data protection standards and be prepared to update or replace them if necessary. This provision is triggered when dealing with legacy agreements, and it requires organizations to assess their compliance status without assuming they automatically meet current GDPR requirements.

Art.97.business_description: Article 97 requires the European Commission to evaluate and review the GDPR and report its findings to the European Parliament and the Council every four years, starting from May 25, 2020. This means that organizations should be aware that the GDPR is subject to periodic review, which may lead to changes or updates in the regulation. However, this article does not impose any direct obligations on controllers or processors. Instead, it ensures that the GDPR remains effective and relevant over time. Organizations should stay informed about these reports as they may influence future compliance requirements.

Art.97.1.business_description: This article mandates that the European Commission must evaluate and review the GDPR every four years, starting from 25 May 2020. The purpose is to assess how well the regulation is functioning and whether any updates are needed. For organizations, this means staying informed about potential changes to the GDPR that could affect compliance requirements. While the article itself does not impose direct actions on controllers or processors, it highlights the importance of monitoring regulatory updates and being prepared to adapt to any changes that may arise from these evaluations.

Art.98.business_description: Article 98 of the GDPR allows the European Commission to propose changes to other EU laws related to data protection. This is to ensure that all laws are aligned and provide consistent protection for individuals' personal data. For controllers and processors, this means staying informed about potential legislative changes that could impact their data protection obligations. This article is triggered when inconsistencies in data protection laws are identified, prompting the Commission to consider amendments. Organizations are not required to take immediate action but should monitor developments to ensure compliance with any new regulations.

Art.98.1.business_description: Article 98.1 of the GDPR requires the European Commission to review and, if necessary, propose changes to other EU laws related to data protection. This ensures that all laws align and provide consistent protection for individuals' personal data. For controllers and processors, this means staying informed about potential legislative changes that could affect their data protection obligations. It is triggered when inconsistencies are identified between the GDPR and other EU legal acts. Organizations should monitor updates from the Commission to ensure compliance with any new or amended laws. A common misconception is that this article imposes direct obligations on organizations, but it primarily guides legislative actions at the EU level.

Art.99.business_description: Article 99 of the GDPR specifies when the regulation became legally effective. It was published in the Official Journal of the European Union and came into force 20 days later. However, organizations were required to comply with its provisions starting from 25 May 2018. This means that from this date, all data controllers and processors had to ensure their operations were in line with GDPR requirements. A common misconception is that the regulation applied immediately upon publication, but there was a transition period allowing organizations to prepare for compliance.

Art.99.1.business_description: This article specifies when the GDPR becomes legally binding. It is triggered when the GDPR is published in the Official Journal of the European Union. Organizations, including data controllers and processors, need to be aware that the GDPR takes effect 20 days after this publication date. This means they must ensure compliance with its provisions by this time. A common misconception is that the GDPR applies immediately upon publication, but there is a 20-day period before it becomes enforceable. Organizations should use this time to finalize their compliance measures.

Art.99.2.business_description: This article specifies the date when the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) became applicable, which is May 25, 2018. For controllers and processors, this means that from this date, they must comply with all the requirements set out in the GDPR. It is important for organizations to understand that any data processing activities conducted after this date must adhere to GDPR standards. A common misconception is that GDPR compliance was optional before this date, but in reality, it became mandatory for all relevant entities starting on May 25, 2018.
